mechanism_narrative: Sustained growth in real GDP indicates a robust economic expansion, which typically translates into higher corporate earnings expectations and thus positive S&P 500 forward returns over a medium-term horizon. This mechanism suggests that a healthy economy provides a supportive backdrop for equity performance.
evidence_refs: fact:historical correlation of GDP growth and equity markets
failure_condition: If GDPC1 growth is positive for two consecutive quarters, yet the S&P 500 produces negative 20-day returns in more than 50% of such instances over a 5-year rolling window.

mechanism_narrative: A significant and sustained increase in the unemployment rate signals a weakening labor market, which typically precedes or accompanies economic contractions. This leads to lower corporate profits, reduced consumer spending, and consequently negative S&P 500 forward returns over a longer horizon.
candidate_trigger: UNRATE rises by more than 0.5 percentage points over a 3-month period
failure_condition: If UNRATE rises by more than 0.5 percentage points over 3 months, yet the S&P 500 produces positive 60-day returns in more than 40% of such instances during identified recessionary periods.

mechanism_narrative: When the Federal Funds Rate is high and inflation (CPIAUCSL) remains persistently elevated, it indicates a tight monetary policy stance that constrains economic activity and corporate profitability. This combination increases the cost of capital and reduces real demand, leading to negative S&P 500 forward returns.
evidence_refs: fact:monetary policy impact on asset prices
failure_condition: If FEDFUNDS is above 4.0% and CPIAUCSL 3-month annualized change is above 3.0%, yet the S&P 500 produces positive 20-day returns in more than 50% of such periods over a 3-year rolling window.

mechanism_narrative: A strong rebound in housing starts (HOUST) following a period of decline signals renewed consumer and business confidence and investment. This acts as a leading indicator for broader economic recovery and typically drives positive S&P 500 forward returns, especially in the short term.
candidate_trigger: HOUST increases by more than 10% month-over-month after a 3-month cumulative decline
evidence_refs: fact:housing as a leading economic indicator
failure_condition: If HOUST shows a strong rebound as specified, but the S&P 500 delivers negative 5-day returns in more than 60% of such instances within an identified recovery regime.

mechanism_narrative: Accelerating industrial production (INDPRO) indicates robust manufacturing and supply-side activity, suggesting strong corporate output and profitability. This positive economic momentum typically translates into higher investor confidence and drives positive S&P 500 forward returns.
evidence_refs: fact:industrial output correlation with corporate earnings
failure_condition: If INDPRO 3-month annualized growth exceeds 2.0%, yet the S&P 500 produces negative 20-day returns in more than 45% of such periods over any 2-year rolling window.

mechanism_narrative: Consistent growth in non-farm payrolls (PAYEMS) reflects a healthy and expanding labor market. This supports robust consumer spending and corporate revenues, leading to sustained positive S&P 500 forward returns over a longer horizon as economic activity remains strong.
failure_condition: If PAYEMS shows positive month-over-month growth for 6 consecutive months, yet the S&P 500 produces negative 60-day returns in more than 50% of those periods over a 5-year rolling window.

mechanism_narrative: When retail sales (RSAFS) are declining while inflation (CPIAUCSL) remains high, it signifies stagflationary pressures. In this environment, consumer demand is weakening despite rising prices, negatively impacting corporate revenues and profit margins, leading to negative S&P 500 forward returns.
candidate_trigger: RSAFS 3-month annualized change < -1.0% AND CPIAUCSL 3-month annualized change > 4.0%
failure_condition: If RSAFS declines and CPIAUCSL remains high as specified, but the S&P 500 delivers positive 5-day returns in more than 55% of such instances over a 3-year rolling window.

mechanism_narrative: A rapidly rising Federal Funds Rate indicates aggressive monetary tightening by the central bank to combat inflation. This action increases borrowing costs, dampens economic activity, and typically leads to a re-evaluation of equity valuations, resulting in negative S&P 500 forward returns.
candidate_trigger: FEDFUNDS increases by more than 0.75 percentage points over a 3-month period
evidence_refs: fact:Fed rate hikes and market reactions
failure_condition: If FEDFUNDS increases by more than 0.75 percentage points over 3 months, yet the S&P 500 produces positive 20-day returns in more than 40% of such instances during identified tightening cycles.
